subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 8! You already know how to add and subtract whole numbers with confidence. In this unit you will take those skills much further and learn to add and subtract decimal numbers and fractions accurately, even when they look awkward. These are the operations that grown-ups reach for every single day, often without noticing. When Amina works out the change from a 500-shilling note at the Hargeisa market, when Yusuf measures how much water is left in a jerrycan, or when Halima adds up parts of a recipe for iftar during Ramadan, they are all adding and subtracting with decimals and fractions.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 6 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: This unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, copy every worked example onto paper and try it yourself, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a fresh way, or check your working line by line. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that still feels tricky.

Why does this matter so much? Decimals and fractions are the language of measurement, money and sharing. Traders use decimals to give exact change and to weigh goods. Builders use fractions and decimals to cut timber to the right length. Families use them to split food fairly and to plan a budget. Even the pump at a fuel station shows litres as a decimal. When you can add and subtract these numbers without fear, you gain a skill you will use for the rest of your life, and you build the foundation you need for percentages, ratio, and algebra in the units and years to come.

As you work, keep a pencil and squared paper beside you. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading; you must do it. Write the columns, line up the points, and say each step out loud. If you make a mistake, that is not a failure; it is simply a signpost pointing at exactly what to practise next. Always estimate first so that a silly slip cannot hide from you. Every confident mathematician you admire once sat where you sit now, learning these very same ideas one careful step at a time.
Add and subtract decimal numbers using correct place value alignment.
Use trailing zeros so numbers with different numbers of decimal places line up.
Borrow (regroup) correctly across a decimal point in subtraction.
Estimate the answer first to check that your result is reasonable.
Find a common denominator and add or subtract fractions with different denominators.
Add and subtract mixed numbers, including when borrowing one whole is needed.
Simplify answers and convert between improper fractions and mixed numbers.

concepts.title: Decimal Place Value
concepts.explanation: A decimal number is simply an extension of the place value system you already know. Just as whole numbers have ones, tens and hundreds, the places to the right of the decimal point show parts of a whole. Each place is ten times smaller than the one before it: the first place is tenths (1/10), the second is hundredths (1/100), and the third is thousandths (1/1000). Let us read the number 6.075 carefully. The 6 is in the ones place. The 0 is in the tenths place, so it means zero tenths. The 7 is in the hundredths…
concepts.example: One idea will save you from many mistakes: a trailing zero does not change the value of a decimal. 0.3 and 0.30 are exactly the same amount, because three tenths equals thirty hundredths. That is why we are allowed to…

concepts.title: Adding Decimal Numbers
concepts.explanation: The golden rule of decimal addition is line up the decimal points. When the points are lined up vertically, tenths sit under tenths, hundredths under hundredths, and so on, so that you only ever add matching parts. If the numbers have different numbers of decimal places, add trailing zeros so every number has the same length. Then add from right to left, carrying whenever a column reaches ten, exactly as you do with whole numbers. Finally, bring the decimal point straight down into the answer. Worked example: 4.49…
concepts.example: Worked example with different lengths: 13.26 + 17.604. The first number has two decimal places and the second has three, so write 13.26 as 13.260 by adding one trailing zero. Now both have three places. Add thousandths:…

concepts.title: Subtracting Decimal Numbers
concepts.explanation: Subtracting decimals uses the same alignment as addition, but you must take extra care with borrowing, also called regrouping. Line up the points, add trailing zeros so both numbers have the same number of decimal places, then subtract from right to left. When a digit on top is too small to subtract from, borrow one from the place to its left, which is worth ten in the current column. Worked example: 10.00 − 7.58. Write 10 as 10.00 so both numbers have two decimal places. Start at the hundredths: 0 − 8 will not…
concepts.example: If borrowing across several zeros feels confusing, try the regrouping picture. Rewrite 10.00 as 9 + 0.9 + 0.10. Now subtract place by place: the ones give 9 − 7 = 2, the tenths give 0.9 − 0.5 = 0.4, and the hundredths…

concepts.title: Estimating to Check Decimals
concepts.explanation: A good mathematician estimates before calculating. An estimate is a quick, sensible guess found by rounding each number to something easy, usually the nearest whole number. It is not the final answer; it is a guard that warns you if your careful calculation has gone badly wrong. If your worked answer is far from your estimate, stop and check for a misplaced decimal point or a lost trailing zero. For example, to add 45.8 + 123.456 you might estimate 46 + 123 = 169. When your column method gives 169.256, the…
concepts.example: Estimation is especially powerful with money. Suppose Omar is buying goods costing 4.85, 9.20 and 6.95 shillings. Rounding each to the nearest whole gives 5 + 9 + 7 = 21, so he knows to expect a little over twenty…

concepts.title: Why Fractions Need a Common Denominator
concepts.explanation: Adding and subtracting fractions is a little more thoughtful than working with whole numbers, because a fraction describes parts of a whole, and those parts can be different sizes. The denominator (bottom number) tells you into how many equal pieces one whole has been cut, and the numerator (top number) tells you how many of those pieces you have. You can only add or subtract fractions directly when the pieces are the same size, that is, when the denominators are equal. Imagine adding 1/2 of a mango to 1/3 of a…
concepts.example: How do you find a common denominator? The most reliable way is the lowest common multiple (LCM) of the two denominators. List the multiples of each denominator and pick the smallest number that appears in both lists.…

concepts.title: Adding and Subtracting Fractions
concepts.explanation: Once both fractions share a common denominator, the work becomes straightforward: convert each fraction to an equivalent fraction with that denominator, then add or subtract only the numerators and keep the denominator the same. Finish by simplifying the answer and, if it is an improper fraction, changing it to a mixed number. Worked example (addition): 2/5 + 3/4. The multiples of 5 are 5, 10, 15, 20 and the multiples of 4 are 4, 8, 12, 16, 20, so the common denominator is 20. Convert: 2/5 = (2×4)/(5×4) = 8/20,…
concepts.example: Worked example (subtraction): 5/6 − 1/4. The multiples of 6 are 6, 12, 18 and the multiples of 4 are 4, 8, 12, so the common denominator is 12. Convert: 5/6 = 10/12 and 1/4 = 3/12. Subtract the numerators: 10/12 − 3/12…

methods.title: How to add or subtract decimals
steps: Estimate the answer by rounding each number.
Write the numbers with points lined up; add trailing zeros so they match.
Work right to left, carrying (for adding) or borrowing (for subtracting).
Bring the decimal point straight down and check against your estimate.

methods.title: How to add or subtract fractions
steps: Find a common denominator (the lowest common multiple of the denominators).
Convert each fraction to an equivalent fraction with that denominator.
Add or subtract the numerators; keep the denominator the same.
Simplify, and change any improper fraction to a mixed number.

methods.title: How to subtract mixed numbers with borrowing
methods.example: Maryam buys items costing 12.75, 8.50 and 6.25 shillings. What is the total?
steps: Give both fractions a common denominator.
If the top fraction is smaller, borrow one whole (as denominator/denominator).
Subtract the whole numbers, then the fractions.
Or convert both to improper fractions to avoid borrowing entirely.

methods.title: Add fractions with different denominators
steps: Solution: The LCM of 3 and 6 is 6.
Convert: 1/3 = 2/6, and 1/6 stays as 1/6.
Add: 2/6 + 1/6 = 3/6 = 1/2 after simplifying.

methods.title: Subtract fractions
steps: Solution: Common denominator 6.
Convert: 2/3 = 4/6 and 1/6 stays.
Check with decimals: 0.67 − 0.17 = 0.5.

methods.title: Add mixed numbers
steps: Solution: Add wholes: 1 + 2 = 3.
Add fractions with denominator 4: 1/2 = 2/4, so 2/4 + 1/4 = 3/4.

workedExamples.title: Add decimals of different lengths
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Write 45.8 as 45.800 so both numbers have three decimal places. Estimate first: 46 + 123 = 169. Add right to left: thousandths 0+6=6; hundredths 0+5=5; tenths 8+4=12, write 2 carry 1; ones 5+3+1=9; tens 4+2=6; hundreds 0+1=1. Answer: 169.256, close to the estimate of 169. Correct.

workedExamples.title: Subtract across zeros
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Write 20 as 20.00. Estimate: 20 − 14 = 6. Subtract with borrowing: hundredths 0−5 borrow to give 5; tenths become 9−6=3; ones 9−3=6; tens 1−1=0. Answer: 6.35, close to the estimate of 6. Correct.

workedExamples.title: A shopping total
workedExamples.prompt: Maryam buys items costing 12.75, 8.50 and 6.25 shillings. What is the total?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: All have two decimal places, so line them up. 12.75 + 8.50 = 21.25, then 21.25 + 6.25 = 27.50 shillings. Estimate: 13 + 9 + 6 = 28, which confirms the answer.

workedExamples.title: Add fractions with different denominators
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The LCM of 3 and 6 is 6. Convert: 1/3 = 2/6, and 1/6 stays as 1/6. Add: 2/6 + 1/6 = 3/6 = 1/2 after simplifying. Check: 1/3 ≈ 0.33 and 1/6 ≈ 0.17, sum ≈ 0.5. Correct.

workedExamples.title: Subtract fractions
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Common denominator 6. Convert: 2/3 = 4/6 and 1/6 stays. Subtract: 4/6 − 1/6 = 3/6 = 1/2. Check with decimals: 0.67 − 0.17 = 0.5. Correct.

workedExamples.title: Add mixed numbers
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Add wholes: 1 + 2 = 3. Add fractions with denominator 4: 1/2 = 2/4, so 2/4 + 1/4 = 3/4. Combine: 3 + 3/4 = 3 3/4.

workedExamples.title: Subtract mixed numbers with borrowing
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Use a common denominator of 4: 3 1/2 = 3 2/4 and 1 3/4 stays. Since 2/4 is smaller than 3/4, borrow one whole: 3 2/4 becomes 2 6/4. Now subtract: wholes 2 − 1 = 1, fractions 6/4 − 3/4 = 3/4. Answer: 1 3/4. Check by improper fractions: 14/4 − 7/4 = 7/4 = 1 3/4. Correct.

workedExamples.title: A multi-step mixture
workedExamples.prompt: A tank holds 45.5 litres. First 12.75 litres is removed, then 8.25 litres is added. How much is in the tank now?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Step 1: 45.5 − 12.75 = 32.75 litres. Step 2: 32.75 + 8.25 = 41.0 litres. Estimate: 46 − 13 + 8 = 41, which matches. Correct.

workedExamples.title: Find the missing amount
workedExamples.prompt: Hassan starts with 50.00 shillings. He spends 12.75, then 8.50, then 15.25. How much is left?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Add the spending: 12.75 + 8.50 + 15.25 = 36.50. Then subtract from the start: 50.00 − 36.50 = 13.50 shillings. Estimate: 50 − 37 = 13, which confirms it.

workedExamples.title: Subtract mixed numbers with borrowing
workedExamples.prompt: Subtract mixed numbers with borrowing: a) 3 1/2 − 1 3/4 b) 3 1/4 − 1 2/3

activities.title: Market Receipt Challenge
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and a few household items with pretend prices.
steps: Give five items 'shilling' prices with two decimal places, such as 3.45 and 12.80.
Choose any three items and add their prices, lining up the decimal points carefully.
Pretend to pay with a round note (for example 50.00) and work out the change by subtracting.
Ask a family member to check your total and your change against a calculator.
This trains the exact decimal skills you use every time you shop.

activities.title: Fraction Recipe Kitchen
activities.materials: a measuring cup (or a marked bottle) and water, rice or flour.
steps: Write a simple 'recipe' that uses fractions, such as 1/2 cup plus 1/3 cup.
Measure each amount, then work out the total by finding a common denominator.
Now pour some back and record how much is left as a subtraction of fractions.
Check your written answer against what you actually see in the cup.

activities.title: Decimal War (A Card Game)
activities.materials: number cards or slips of paper.
steps: Each player makes a decimal number with two places from three digit cards, such as 4.72.
Both players add their two most recent numbers; the larger total wins the round.
For a subtraction round, the larger number minus the smaller wins if the difference is over 1.
Keep score over ten rounds. Estimating quickly helps you decide who is ahead.
This game builds speed and confidence with lining up decimal points.

activities.title: Jerrycan Water Investigation
activities.materials: a marked container and access to water (do this outside).
steps: Fill the container and record the starting amount as a decimal, such as 5.00 litres.
Pour some out for a task and measure what you removed, for example 1.75 litres.
Predict how much is left by subtracting, then measure to check your prediction.
Repeat with different amounts and see how close your predictions get each time.

activities.title: The Missing Number Puzzle (Family Game)
activities.materials: paper and pencil, and one other person.
steps: One person writes an addition such as 4.5 + ___ = 7.2 and hides the missing number.
The other person finds it by subtracting: 7.2 − 4.5 = 2.7.
Swap roles and try it with fractions, such as 3/4 + ___ = 1 whole.
Talk about how addition and subtraction undo each other.

activities.title: Rainfall Data Project (Investigation)
activities.materials: paper, and imagined or recorded daily amounts.
steps: Write down a decimal 'rainfall' amount in centimetres for each of five days.
Add all five amounts to find the weekly total, lining up the points.
Work out how far the total is above or below five 'average' days of 2.5 cm each.
Draw a simple bar for each day and label it with its decimal amount.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand decimals or fractions best? What was the trickiest part, and how did you get past it? What would you like more practice with? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Decimal Alignment Rule
rules.text: Line up the decimal points, not the last digits. Fill gaps with trailing zeros so every number is the same length. Bring the decimal point straight down into the answer.

rules.title: Fraction Rule
rules.text: You can only add or subtract fractions with the same denominator. Find a common denominator (usually the lowest common multiple). Add or subtract the numerators only; keep the denominator the same; then simplify.

rules.title: Estimate-First Rule
rules.text: Round each number to a whole number and estimate before you calculate. If your exact answer is far from the estimate, check for a misplaced point.

terms: A zero added on the right to help line up
Top number / bottom number of a fraction
A shared bottom number for two fractions
Lowest number both denominators divide into
A whole number and a fraction, e.g. 2 1/3
Numerator not less than denominator, e.g. 7/3
Write a fraction in lowest terms
commonMistakes: Divide top and bottom by 2 to get 3/4
Dropping the point in the answer
Bring the point straight down first

Which is larger: (2/3 + 1/4) or (3/4 + 1/6)? Show your calculations.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Both equal 11/12, so they are equal.

reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Once both fractions share a common denominator, the work becomes straightforward: convert each fraction to an equivalent fraction with that denominator, then add or subtract only the numerators and keep the denominator the same. Finish by simplifying the answer and, if it is an improper fraction, changing it to a mixed number. Worked example (addition): 2/5 + 3/4. The multiples of 5 are 5, 10, 15, 20 and the multiples of 4 are 4, 8, 12, 16, 20, so the common denominator is 20. Convert: 2/5 = (2×4)/(5×4) = 8/20,…

questions.question: What does “Decimal point” mean?
options: A zero added on the right to help line up
Top number / bottom number of a fraction
questions.hint: Use the Unit 8 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Decimal point means Dot separating whole from parts.

questions.question: What does “Tenths / Hundredths” mean?
options: A zero added on the right to help line up
Top number / bottom number of a fraction
A shared bottom number for two fractions
questions.hint: Use the Unit 8 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Tenths / Hundredths means First and second decimal places.

questions.question: What does “Trailing zero” mean?
options: A zero added on the right to help line up
Top number / bottom number of a fraction
A shared bottom number for two fractions
Lowest number both denominators divide into
questions.answer: A zero added on the right to help line up
questions.hint: Use the Unit 8 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Trailing zero means A zero added on the right to help line up.

selfAssessment: I can this unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, copy every worked example onto paper and try it yourself, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a fresh way, or check your working line by line. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that still feels tricky.

I can why does this matter so much? Decimals and fractions are the language of measurement, money and sharing. Traders use decimals to give exact change and to weigh goods. Builders use fractions and decimals to cut timber to the right length. Families use them to split food fairly and to plan a budget. Even the pump at a fuel station shows litres as a decimal. When you can add and subtract these numbers without fear, you gain a skill you will use for the rest of your life, and you build the foundation you need for percentages, ratio, and algebra in the units and years to come.

I can as you work, keep a pencil and squared paper beside you. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading; you must do it. Write the columns, line up the points, and say each step out loud. If you make a mistake, that is not a failure; it is simply a signpost pointing at exactly what to practise next. Always estimate first so that a silly slip cannot hide from you. Every confident mathematician you admire once sat where you sit now, learning these very same ideas one careful step at a time.
I can add and subtract decimal numbers using correct place value alignment.
I can use trailing zeros so numbers with different numbers of decimal places line up.
I can borrow (regroup) correctly across a decimal point in subtraction.
I can estimate the answer first to check that your result is reasonable.
I can find a common denominator and add or subtract fractions with different denominators.
